29: peitharchein dei theo mallon e anthropois (peitharchein dei theō mallon ē anthrōpois): 'we must obey God rather than human beings'. dei is divine necessity, not preference — the same dei that drove Jesus's entire ministry (Luke 2:49; Acts 4:43; Acts 9:22). peitharchein (obey authority) is the compound of peitho (persuade/trust) + archē (ruling authority), carrying the nuance that legitimate authority deserves compliance — but God's authority supersedes all human authority structures.
1: Hananias (Ananias) and Sappheira (Sapphira) — both Hebrew names. Luke records their names, unlike many anonymous figures in Acts, signaling that this event had historical specificity in community memory. The narrative introduces the community's first internal moral failure — not external persecution but internal deception. The threat to the church from within is as grave as the threat from the Sanhedrin without.
2: enosphisato (enosphisato, 'he embezzled/misappropriated'): the same word used in the LXX of Joshua 7:1 (enosphisato) for Achan's misappropriation of devoted things from Jericho's spoils. The resonance is almost certainly deliberate: Achan's sin introduced covenant violation into Israel immediately after entry into the land; Ananias's sin introduces covenant violation into the church immediately after Pentecost. Both disrupted a new covenantal beginning; both were exposed by divine revelation.
3: eplērōsen ho Satanas tēn kardian sou ('Satan has filled your heart'): the contrast with Spirit-filling is explicit — Pentecost filled the community with the Holy Spirit (Acts 2:4; Acts 4:31); Ananias has been filled by Satan. The same verb (plēroō), the same location (the heart), the opposite agent and direction. Deception is not merely moral failure but a form of demonic collaboration. pseudesthai ('to lie'): lying to the Spirit is lying to God (Acts 5:4) — Peter's equation affirms the Spirit's full divinity.
4: ouchi menon soi emenen kai prathen en sē exousia hypērchen: Peter's double assertion — while it remained unsold it was yours; after it was sold the proceeds were at your disposal — confirms that the Jerusalem community did not require communal property. The sin was not keeping the money but lying about having given it all. The voluntary nature of the sharing is essential to understanding the community's economic ethic: it was genuine gift, not tax.
5: ekpneō (ekpneusas, 'he breathed his last/expired'): Luke uses clinical vocabulary — the same verb he uses for Jesus's death in Luke 23:46. The sudden death is not reported as miraculous in the ordinary sense; it is a divine judicial act. phobos megas ('great fear'): the fear that follows Ananias's death and then Sapphira's (Acts 5:11) is not terror but awe — the recognition that the community inhabits a space where divine holiness is operative and cannot be trifled with.
6: synesteilantes (systeilan, 'wrapping/shrouding'): the young men (hoi neōteroi) act immediately without deliberation. The speed of the burial is consistent with Jewish practice (burial before sundown) and adds to the solemnity of the narrative — there is no interval for appeal or reconsideration. Luke's brevity here reflects the event's shock value in community memory.
7: hōs hōrōn triōn diastēma ('after about three hours'): Luke's chronological precision creates dramatic irony — Sapphira has been going about her day unaware of her husband's death, and comes in after three hours to face Peter's question. The interval is long enough that the burial is complete but short enough that she still expects to reunite with her husband. Luke's narrative technique here produces the same tension as a trial scene.
8: Peter's question — tosoutou to chorion apedosthe ('did you sell the land for so much?') — is not a request for information but a forensic trap. Peter already knows the answer (through the Spirit's revelation, Acts 5:3). He gives Sapphira the opportunity to tell the truth; her confirmation of the lie is her own self-condemnation. The interrogation follows the pattern of divine testing in the OT (Genesis 3:9-13; Acts 4:9), where God questions not from ignorance but to expose what the heart already holds.
9: peirazo (peirasai, 'to test'): Peter's charge — 'why did you conspire to test the Spirit of the Lord?' — places the Ananias and Sapphira incident in the category of Israel's wilderness murmuring and testing of God (Exodus 17:2, 7; Psalms 78:18, 56; Acts 95:9; Acts 106:14). Testing the Spirit of the Lord was the characteristic sin of the wilderness generation that died before reaching the land; the same pattern is now enacted at the beginning of the new covenant community.
10: Sapphira's immediate death (epesen de parachrēma pros tous podas autou kai exepsyxen, 'immediately she fell at his feet and expired') parallels her husband's death structurally. Luke notes both that she fell 'at his feet' (cf. the apostles' feet as locus of authority in Acts 4:35, 37) and that the same young men came and buried her beside her husband. The symmetry is deliberate: the couple acted together in deception; they died together in judgment.
11: ekklēsia (ekklēsia): this is the first unambiguous use of ekklēsia in Acts (the word appears in variant readings at 2:47 but is textually uncertain there). The word meant the civic assembly of citizens in Hellenistic cities but carried OT weight as the LXX translation of qahal (the assembly of Israel). Luke's application of ekklēsia to the messianic community identifies them as the renewed assembly of Israel, the covenant people gathered in the new exodus.
12: sēmeia kai terata polla ('many signs and wonders'): the third summary of apostolic miracles (Acts 2:43; Acts 4:30; Acts 5:12). Luke's repetition establishes a rhythm: the word is proclaimed, the Spirit acts in power, the community grows, opposition arises. Stoa tou Solomōnos ('Solomon's Porch'): the eastern colonnade of the temple complex (cf. John 10:23); the apostles used a public, recognizable location, not a private house, for teaching.
13: oudeis etolma kollasthai autois ('no one else dared to join them'): kollaō (attach oneself, join closely) is the same word used for the community's adherence to one another (Acts 5:36 in a different context). The awe following the Ananias and Sapphira judgment created a selective barrier: casual seekers kept their distance while those who genuinely believed were added (Acts 5:14). Luke presents holy fear as a purifying filter on membership, not a deterrent to genuine conversion.
14: prosetithento pisteuontes tō kyriō ('believers were added to the Lord'): the community's growth is consistently described as addition to the Lord (Acts 2:47; Acts 5:14; Acts 11:24) — not merely to the group — signaling that the community's center of gravity is personal relationship to the risen Christ. The explicit mention of both men and women (andrōn te kai gynaikōn) after Pentecost's inclusive outpouring (Joel 2:28-29) is Luke's narrative confirmation that gender is not a barrier to membership.
15: hina erchomenou Petrou kan hē skia episkiasē ('so that even Peter's shadow might fall on some of them'): Luke does not affirm whether the shadow healings worked; he reports the crowd's expectation. The pattern echoes the healings through Paul's handkerchiefs and aprons in Acts 19:12 — the divine power operating through physical media associated with the apostle. Both passages reflect popular faith that God worked through proximity to his agents, as he worked through Elisha's bones (2 Kings 13:21).
16: ochloi ('crowds'): the geographic expansion — from Jerusalem to surrounding towns — follows the Acts 1:8 pattern. The mission radiates outward naturally, not yet through intentional missionary strategy but through the reputation of the apostolic ministry. ochloumenous hypo pneumatōn akathartōn ('tormented by unclean spirits'): Luke's healing summaries always include exorcism alongside physical healing, maintaining the integrated Synoptic pattern of Jesus's ministry.
17: anastropheis zēlou (plēsthēnai zēlou, 'filled with jealousy'): the same term — filled (plēroō) — used for Spirit-filling now describes the jealousy that animates the high priest. The bitter irony is structural: the high priest, who should lead the community in discerning God's work, is filled with the anti-Spirit of jealousy while the apostles are filled with the Holy Spirit. Luke's vocabulary creates a pointed comparison.
18: epebalan tas cheiras ('laid their hands on'): the hostile laying on of hands contrasts with the community's gesture of commissioning and blessing (Acts 6:6; Acts 13:3). The same physical act carries opposite meanings depending on the agent and intention. Luke's narrative sophistication regularly exploits these lexical contrasts to highlight the divided response to the apostolic mission.
19: angelos de Kyriou ('an angel of the Lord'): the divine rescue pattern — imprisonment followed by miraculous release — is established here (cf. Acts 12:7-11; Acts 16:26). Luke presents the angel as divine agent, not metaphor; the detail about opening locked doors (Acts 5:23) makes the miraculous nature explicit. The angel's message is not merely release but re-commission: 'go and speak in the temple all the words of this life' (Acts 5:20).
20: ta rhēmata tēs zōēs tautēs ('the words of this life'): the phrase 'this life' (tautēs) refers to the resurrection life — the eschatological life inaugurated by Christ's resurrection that is now available and is the content of the apostolic proclamation. The command returns the apostles immediately to the place of danger (the temple courts) rather than directing them to safety — witness continues in the theater of opposition.
21: gerousion (gerousian): a near-synonym for the Sanhedrin, literally 'assembly of elders' (cf. the Hebrew ziqnei Israel). Luke's doubling — 'Sanhedrin and all the senate of the sons of Israel' — may indicate the full formal convening of all associated leadership bodies, or may be literary intensification to indicate the comprehensive nature of the official response. Either way, it signals the maximum institutional weight arrayed against the apostles.
22: The officers' discovery of the empty prison — euron autous ou en tē phylakē ('they did not find them in the prison') — is narrated without explanation at this point; Luke delays the angelic rescue account structurally (it is implied but not yet stated). The sequence creates a moment of dramatic suspense: the officials know the prison is secure (Acts 5:23) but the apostles are absent. The resolution requires the theophanic framework of angelic intervention.
23: meta pasēs aspaleias kekleismenon ('locked with all security'): the guards' report emphasizes the physical security of the prison — doors locked, guards in place — to make the angelic release undeniable. Luke's detail serves an apologetic function: the release cannot be explained by bribery, guard negligence, or official connivance. The only available explanation is the miraculous intervention that the narrative has already identified (Acts 5:19).
24: diēporoun peri autōn ('they were perplexed about them'): diaporeō (be thoroughly perplexed) is Luke's term for the cognitive disruption that miraculous events produce in observers (cf. Luke 9:7; Acts 2:12; Acts 10:17). The officials cannot fit the data into their interpretive framework; the prison is secure but empty. Luke presents such perplexity as the appropriate preliminary to genuine inquiry — it opens the question that only the gospel can answer.
25: The report — 'the men you put in prison are standing in the temple courts teaching the people' — is narrated at maximum dramatic compression. Luke does not explain the officers' emotional response; it is implied by the contrast: they locked the apostles away, and now find them publicly doing the exact thing they were imprisoned for doing. The apostles' obedience to the angel's command (Acts 5:20) is confirmed by the report.
26: ou meta bias ('without force/violence'): the captain's restraint reflects the same popular constraint noted in Acts 4:21 — the authorities cannot act against the apostles openly without risking popular backlash. Luke's 'fear of being stoned' is a recognition that the crowd's sympathy for the apostles served as providential protection. The same dynamic will reappear in Acts 21-26, where Paul navigates Roman and Jewish legal systems partly because of the crowd's divided loyalties.
27: The second appearance before the Sanhedrin parallels and escalates the first (Acts 4). The formal setting — estēsan en tō synedriō ('they stood them in the Sanhedrin') — and the high priest's interrogation (eperōtēsen autous) reprises the earlier tribunal scene. Luke's narrative is building a pattern: the apostles are repeatedly arrested, repeatedly released, and repeatedly more bold, enacting the Ps 2 scenario of opposition that cannot ultimately prevail.
28: eplērōkate tēn Ierousalēm tēs didachēs hymōn ('you have filled Jerusalem with your teaching'): the accusation ironically confirms what Luke has been narrating — the gospel has indeed filled Jerusalem. The council's complaint is Luke's progress report. epagagein eph' hēmas to haima tou anthrōpou toutou ('bring this man's blood upon us'): an implicit acknowledgment of guilt; they recognize that the apostles are accusing them of Jesus's death, which they cannot flatly deny.
30: kremesantes epi xylou (kremasantes epi xylou, 'hanging on a tree'): the phrase evokes Deuteronomy 21:23 ('anyone who is hung on a pole is under God's curse'). Paul makes the connection explicit in Galatians 3:13 ('Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written: Cursed is everyone who is hung on a tree'). Luke's language here implies the same Deuteronomic background: Jesus bore the covenant curse, which is why God raised him from the dead rather than leaving him under it.
31: archēgon kai sōtēra (archēgon kai sōtēra, 'Pioneer and Savior'): the archēgos Christology continues from Acts 3:15. A sōtēr (savior) in Hellenistic political usage was typically a king or general who delivered a city from enemies; Luke applies it to Jesus as the deliverer from sin and death. The pairing — Pioneer who goes first, Savior who brings others through — is Luke's comprehensive summary of Christ's redemptive work. metanoia and aphesis ('repentance' and 'forgiveness') are the twin gifts his exaltation makes available.
32: martyres again (cf. Acts 1:8, 22; Acts 2:32; Acts 3:15; Acts 4:33; Acts 5:32): Luke's accumulating use of the witness-vocabulary emphasizes that apostolic testimony is grounded in personal encounter, not theological speculation. The Spirit's co-witness (kai to Pneuma to Hagion ho edōken ho theos tois peitharchousin autō) is significant: the Spirit testifies alongside the human witnesses, making the testimony doubly grounded — in eyewitness experience and in ongoing divine confirmation.
33: dieprionto (dieprionto, 'they were cut to the quick/sawn asunder'): the violent metaphor — literally 'to be sawn through' — describes the council's furious reaction. The word appears again in 7:54 at Stephen's speech. The council's rage stands in sharp contrast to the crowd's receptive fear (Acts 5:11) and prepares for Gamaliel's moderating intervention.
34: Gamaliel: identified as a Pharisee and nomodidaskalos (teacher of the law), honored by all the people. The Gamaliel of Acts is almost certainly the Gamaliel I mentioned in later rabbinic tradition as a leading sage; Paul identifies himself as trained at Gamaliel's feet (Acts 22:3). Luke presents the Pharisees and Sadducees as divided in their response to the apostles — the Sadducees drive the persecution, while this leading Pharisee advocates restraint.
35: Gamaliel's speech addresses the Sanhedrin with the formal address andres Israēlitai ('men of Israel') and immediately counsels caution (prosechete heautois, 'take care for yourselves'). His appeal is to pragmatic historical precedent rather than theological principle — he does not assess whether the apostles' claims are true but whether suppression is strategically wise. Luke's inclusion of this speech is not a straightforward endorsement of Gamaliel's reasoning but a narrative device showing that even the council's own members could not unify against the apostles.
36: The Theudas reference creates a famous historical difficulty: Josephus mentions a Theudas who led a movement around AD 44-46, after the events of Acts 5. Either Luke's dating is imprecise, there were multiple figures named Theudas, or Luke has inverted the chronological sequence in constructing Gamaliel's speech. The historical problem does not undermine Luke's theological point but is worth noting for honest engagement with the text.
37: Judas the Galilean led a tax revolt at the census of AD 6 (Josephus, Antiquities 18.1.1), establishing the anti-Roman Zealot tradition. Gamaliel's point — that both movements dissolved when their leaders died — is historically accurate for Judas. His argument has the form of an analogy from recent history: if this is of human origin, it will follow the same pattern; if from God, you cannot stop it.
38: aphete autous ('leave them alone'): Gamaliel's counsel is restraint, not openness. His logic — ei estin ex anthrōpōn hē boulē hautē ē to ergon touto ('if this plan or work is from men') — is pragmatic probability-assessment, not theological discernment. The argument works as a rhetorical check on the council's anger without requiring Gamaliel to assess the apostles' claims. Luke does not endorse Gamaliel's methodology (it would apply equally to any movement that survived) but uses it to show that even the council's own traditions argued against violent suppression.
39: mēpote kai theomachoi heurethēte ('you might even be found fighting against God'): the theomachos (God-fighter) was a category of hubris in both Hellenistic and Jewish thought — the human who presumes to oppose divine action. Gamaliel's warning is the sharpest edge of his argument: if this is God's work, suppression is not merely futile but blasphemous. The council cannot be certain which it is; prudence therefore counsels restraint. The irony for Luke's reader is that it is God's work, making the council's subsequent flogging (Acts 5:40) a confirmed case of theomachic overreach.
40: mastigōsantes ('having flogged them'): the Sanhedrin did not follow Gamaliel's counsel completely — they flogged the apostles before releasing them. The Jewish judicial flogging was Deuteronomy 25:2-3's thirty-nine stripes (Paul refers to receiving this five times, 2 Corinthians 11:24). The flogging was intended as both punishment and deterrent; its actual effect (Acts 5:41) is the inverse of what was intended.
41: chairontes ('rejoicing'): the apostles' response to flogging is characterized by Luke as genuine joy, not stoic endurance. The grounds — hoti katēxiōthēsan atimasthēnai hyper tou onomatos ('that they were counted worthy to suffer dishonor for the name') — invert the normal honor-shame calculus. In a society where public shame was devastating, the apostles experience their dishonor as a form of honor — sharing in their Lord's suffering, as Jesus had promised (Luke 6:22-23; Acts 21:12-13). The beatitude is being enacted.
42: kath' hēmeran ('day by day') + kat' oikon ('from house to house') + en tō hierō ('in the temple'): Luke's closing summary describes the rhythm of apostolic ministry — daily, in both public space (temple) and private space (homes), never stopping (ouk epaūonto). The word 'teaching' (didaskontes) and 'proclaiming the good news' (euangelizomenoi) are paired; both catechesis of believers and evangelism of outsiders are continuous. The pattern will be disrupted only by the Hellenist controversy of Acts 6.
